steps.n: This is a question word that turns the statement 'it came to you' into the question 'has it come to you?'
Added sukun (ْ) on the ل to show where to stop when reading
The letter ي is a weak letter, and it changes in different verb conjugations. Here, it's changed to a short vowel.
Added the alif sign (ٰ) after ى to show it's a long vowel sound

steps.m: he came to you (connected to هَلْ - 'has')
steps.n: Added كَ at the end to show the action was directed 'to you'. This word follows هَلْ (has) to ask 'has (it) come to you?'

steps.m: to tell (a story/news), to happen, to occur

steps.n: Changes from a verb (to tell) into a noun (speech/narrative)
Added damma at the end to show this is the subject of the verb أَتَىٰكَ (has come to you)
Changes from verb 'to cover' to noun meaning 'that which covers/overwhelms'
Added ال at beginning to make it specific and ِ at end to show it follows another word
Changes the root letters into a simple noun meaning 'face' by adding the vowels
Changes from singular 'face' to plural 'faces' by changing middle and end vowels to 'u' sound
Adds tanween damma to show this is the subject being described as humble/cast down by خٰشِعَةٌ
Connected the root letters with vowel marks to form the basic noun
Added fatha at the end to show this word is being used as an object or time expression

steps.m: on that day (when faces will be humble)
steps.n: Added ئِذٍ to specify 'that specific day' being discussed. This word connects to خٰشِعَةٌ (humble) which describes what happens on this day.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the past tense verb
Changed to active participle form to describe the state of the faces
Added feminine marker because it describes وُجُوهٌ which is feminine plural
Added two dots on top (tanween) to show this word describes 'faces' (وُجُوهٌ) and matches its ending
Active participle showing who does the action. This word pairs with نَاصِبَةٌ (exhausted) to describe faces that will be working and toiling on the Day of Judgment.
Adds feminine ending to show this refers to a female worker
Adding tanween makes it an adjective describing وُجُوهٌ (faces) from earlier in the passage
Added shadda on first letter and connected letters with fatha marks
Changes from verb (action) to noun (doer of action)
Makes the word feminine - describes a female who is doing hard work
Adds two dots above the last letter to show it's indefinite
Added vowels (fatha) to make it past tense 'he' form
Added تَ at beginning to show 'she' is doing the action in present tense. The sukun (ْ) helps with pronunciation. This verb connects to نَارًا حَامِيَةً (blazing fire) which comes after it.

steps.m: she will burn/roast (subject of نَارًا حَامِيَةً)
steps.n: The alif maksura (ىٰ) was already part of the original word - it's just written differently in the two versions but represents the same future-tense verb meaning 'she will burn'. Both forms are the same word.
The weak letter و changes to ا, which is common in Arabic. The meaning narrows to specifically 'fire' rather than light in general.

steps.m: a fire (object of تَصْلَىٰ - will enter/burn in)
steps.n: The ًا at the end shows this is the object receiving the action of تَصْلَىٰ (entering/burning). It means 'a fire' rather than 'the fire'.
Adding vowel marks to show this is a past tense verb about becoming hot
Changed to active participle pattern showing ongoing state
Added feminine marker to describe نَارًا (fire) which is feminine
Added tanween to match the word it describes (نَارًا - fire). Both words end in ًا because they go together.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form basic past tense verb
Added تُ at start to make it 'is given drink' (present tense), and changed vowels to show someone else is doing the action

steps.m: she/it is given drink (passive present) (followed by مِنْ)
steps.n: Added alif maksura at the end to show this connects to the following word مِنْ (from)

This is a basic preposition that shows where something comes from. In this ayah, it connects the verb 'they will be given to drink' (تُسْقَىٰ) with its source 'a spring' (عَيْنٍ), showing where the drink comes from.

steps.m: spring (of boiling water) (object of تُسْقَىٰ مِنْ - they will be given to drink from)
steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha and sukoon to form the noun. This word is the object that follows مِنْ (from) in the phrase 'they will be given to drink from a spring'

steps.m: a spring (of boiling water) (object of تُسْقَىٰ مِنْ - they will be given to drink from)
steps.n: Adds two dots under the ن (tanween) to make it mean 'a spring' instead of 'the spring'
Changed from basic root to describe something that is very hot right now (like boiling water)
Added ي to create intensive form showing permanent quality
Added ة to make word feminine to match عَيْنٍ which is feminine
Added tanwin kasra to match the case of عَيْنٍ (spring) that it describes
This is a special verb used for negation
Adding vowels to make it a complete verb meaning 'is not'
Added emphasis by doubling the first letter. This word negates طَعَامٌ (food) in this ayah, saying there definitely is no food.
Added fatha to show the basic preposition meaning

steps.m: for them (refers to disbelievers mentioned in لَّيْسَ)
steps.n: Added هُمْ which shows this applies to a group of males. In this ayah, it refers back to the disbelievers mentioned in the word لَّيْسَ (there is not).

steps.m: to feed, to eat, to taste

steps.n: Added alif after ع to change from action (eating) to the thing itself (food). This word is the subject of لَيْسَ (there is not) in this ayah.
The double-damma (ٌ) shows this is talking about food in general, not a specific food. It's the subject of لَيْسَ (there is not).

This is an exception particle that excludes what comes after it (ضَرِيعٍ - a thorny plant) from what comes before it (طَعَامٌ - food). In this ayah, it's saying they have no food except this thorny plant.
Basic preposition showing that their food comes from a specific source (bitter, thorny plants)
Added sukun mark to show where the word stops
Forms a noun for a specific type of bitter, thorny plant that animals avoid eating
Shows this word is affected by the word مِن (from) that comes before it in the ayah
This is a negative particle that makes the verb يُسْمِنُ (fattens) into 'does not fatten'
The shadda and fatha make the negation stronger, emphasizing that it definitely does not fatten
Added vowels to connect the root letters together
The prefix يُ shows it's happening now/future. The vowel pattern changes it from 'becoming fat' to 'making something fat'. This verb is negated by لَّا (does not) in the ayah.
This is a negative particle that makes the verb يُغْنِى (it nourishes) into 'it does not nourish'

steps.m: and not (connects to previous negative لَّا)
steps.n: The وَ means 'and', connecting this negative with the previous لَّا to show multiple things that are not true
Added vowels to connect the root letters and show this happened in the past

Added يُ at start to show present tense and making something happen to others. The ي at end becomes ى because it's at the end of a word. This verb is negated by لَا in the ayah, so it means 'does not make sufficient'.

steps.m: from (connects جُوعٍ 'hunger' to show what doesn't nourish)
steps.n: This is a helper word that shows something comes from or is caused by something else. Here it connects to the word 'hunger' to show what the food doesn't protect from.
Changed from action to the feeling itself by adding damma (ُ) on first letter. This makes it a noun describing the state of being hungry.

steps.m: from hunger (in مِن جُوعٍ - from hunger)
steps.n: Added tanween kasra (ٍ) to show this word comes after مِن (from) in the phrase مِن جُوعٍ (from hunger)
Connected the root letters with basic vowel marks to form the singular noun
Changed to broken plural pattern by adding و and changing vowels to make it mean 'many faces'
Added tanween dammah to show this is the subject of نَّاعِمَةٌ (blissful) in this ayah
Added vowel marks to make the basic noun meaning 'day'
Added fatha at the end to show this word is connected to what comes after it

steps.m: on that day (when faces will be نَّاعِمَةٌ [joyful])
steps.n: Added ئِذٍ to specify 'that day' referring to a specific time being discussed
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Adding shadda on first letter makes the meaning stronger
Adding alif after first letter and kasra under second letter changes it to describe someone/something feeling pleasure
Adding ة makes the word feminine, describing a female/feminine thing that is feeling pleasure
Adding tanween damma shows this is describing the faces (وُجُوهٌ) mentioned in the ayah

steps.m: to strive or work towards a goal

steps.n: Connected the root letters and added vowel marks to make it into a noun meaning the act itself
Added kasra at the end to show this word is owned by something
Added ها at the end to show the effort belongs to her/it
Added لِّ at the beginning to show this is the reason for being رَاضِيَةٌ (pleased/satisfied) in the ayah
Basic past tense verb showing the feeling of being pleased
Changed from past tense verb (he was pleased) to a word describing someone who is pleased - like changing 'ran' to 'runner'
Added feminine marker to match with وُجُوه (faces) which is feminine in Arabic
Added two dots (tanween) above to show this describes 'faces' that are happy
This is a basic preposition showing location. In this ayah, it shows being physically present in a garden.
The ي changes to ى when it comes at the end of a word. This is just a spelling change and doesn't affect the meaning.
Added fatha on first letter and doubled the middle letter to show intensity of covering

steps.m: a garden (because it provides complete cover with its trees)
steps.n: Added ة to make it a noun for a place that provides complete cover, which is what a garden does
Added tanween kasra to show this is the object of the word فِى (in)

steps.m: to rise up or be high
steps.n: The root conveys the concept of height or elevation
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past-tense verb
Changes from verb 'he rose' to adjective meaning 'high'
Added the feminine ending ة because it's describing جَنَّةٍ (garden) which is feminine in Arabic

steps.m: a high, rising (feminine form describing جَنَّةٍ/garden)
steps.n: Added -in sound at the end to match the word 'garden' that it's describing
This is a basic negative particle that negates the verb تَسْمَعُ (you hear) that comes after it in this ayah

steps.m: absolutely no/not at all (negates تَسْمَعُ 'you hear')
steps.n: The shadda (ّ) and fatha (َ) make the negation stronger, emphasizing that you will hear absolutely no idle talk
Added fathas to connect the letters and show this happened in the past

steps.m: you hear (in لَّا تَسْمَعُ - you will not hear)
steps.n: Added تَ at start to show 'you', changed vowels to show present/future tense. This word is part of لَّا تَسْمَعُ meaning 'you will not hear'.
This is a preposition that has no root - it's a basic word showing location or containment
Added ها ('it') to في ('in') to mean 'in it', pointing back to the garden mentioned before
This root is about empty or meaningless speech
Root letters connected with vowels to make a noun meaning 'useless talk'

steps.m: one who engages in empty talk
steps.n: Changes from 'empty talk' to 'one who does empty talk'
Added feminine ending to show one specific instance of empty talk
Added tanween to show it's indefinite (any empty talk) and made it the object of 'you hear' (تَسْمَعُ)

steps.m: in (preposition showing location in جَنَّةٍ عَالِيَةٍ 'lofty garden')
steps.n: Basic preposition showing location. Here it refers to being inside the lofty garden mentioned earlier.
The هَا at the end refers back to the garden (جَنَّة) which is feminine in Arabic
This root is related to eyes and seeing, but also to flowing water
Added the vowels 'fatha' and 'sukun' to the root letters to make the word mean 'spring of water'

steps.m: a spring (flowing in جَنَّةٍ عَالِيَةٍ - an elevated garden)
steps.n: The double-damma (tanwin) shows this is indefinite - referring to any spring. This spring is described as جَارِيَةٌ (flowing) in the next word.
Connected the root letters with fatha to create the basic past tense verb
Changes from a verb (he flowed) to a noun meaning 'one who/which flows'
Added ة to make the word feminine, since it describes عَيْنٌ (spring) which is feminine
Added tanwin damma to show this is indefinite and matches the case of عَيْنٌ (spring) that it describes
Added the kasra vowel mark under ف to show how it's pronounced

steps.m: in it/her (referring to the elevated couches mentioned after)
steps.n: The word في (in) gets ها (meaning 'it/her') attached to the end to mean 'in it', pointing to the couches mentioned next
This is a plural form meaning 'thrones/couches' - the meaning shifted from the emotional root to physical objects that bring comfort and joy
Added damma vowels to create a plural noun meaning comfortable seats or thrones. This word appears in a description of Paradise's furnishings.
Added tanween damma to show this is the subject being described by the word مَّرْفُوعَةٌ (raised/elevated) in this ayah
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb
Changed from 'he raised' to 'something that was raised' by adding مَ at start and changing middle vowels - this is a common pattern for describing things that received an action
Added ة to make it feminine since it describes سُرُرٌ (thrones/couches) which is feminine in Arabic
Added tanween (double dammah) to make it indefinite - meaning 'a' raised thing rather than 'the' raised thing
Added vowel marks to turn the root letters into a specific cup/drinking vessel
Changed from single cup to many cups by using the special plural pattern أَفْعَال
Added و (and) at start and ٌ to show this is the subject being described by the word مَّوْضُوعَةٌ (placed/set)
Connected the root letters with fatha to make the basic past tense verb
Changes from a verb (he placed) to a noun meaning something that was placed
Makes the word feminine to match what it's describing (cups/أَكْوَابٌ)
Shows this is describing cups (أَكْوَابٌ) in a general way
The small م (shaddah) on top shows the cups are carefully and perfectly placed
Changes from single cushion to multiple cushions

steps.m: and cushions (arranged in rows with مَصْفُوفَةٌ)
steps.n: Adds و at the beginning to mean 'and' and ُ at the end to show this is the subject being described as 'arranged in rows' by the word مَصْفُوفَةٌ

steps.m: to arrange or line up in rows

steps.n: The root 'to arrange in rows' becomes 'arranged in rows' by adding مَ at start and و to show it's been done to the cushions

steps.m: arranged in rows (feminine form for cushions)
steps.n: Added ة to match the feminine word نَمَارِق (cushions) it's describing

steps.m: arranged in rows (feminine, describing the cushions as general items)
steps.n: Added ٌ to show these are general cushions being described, not specific ones

steps.m: to spread out or cover (like a carpet)

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make it a past tense verb
Changed the word pattern to make it mean 'something that is spread out' (like a carpet)
Added ي with shadda to show this is a special, high-quality type of carpet
Added damma to show this is the subject being described as مَبْثُوثَةٌ (spread out)
Added و at beginning to connect this word to what came before it in the verse
Added مَ at start and و before last letter to show this describes something that has been scattered
Makes the passive participle feminine
Makes the word indefinite by adding double dammah
This letter turns a statement into a question, showing surprise or criticism
Adds فَ to show this question follows from something before it

steps.m: Then do they not...? (connecting to يَنظُرُونَ - they look)
steps.n: Adds لا to make the question negative. This combination expresses amazement that people don't look at something obvious. It connects to the verb يَنظُرُونَ (they look) that comes after it.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the past tense verb
The يَ at the start and the ُ marks change it from past to present tense
Adding ونَ makes it plural for 'they'. In this ayah, they are looking at 'the camels' (الْإِبِلِ)
This is a basic preposition showing direction. In this ayah, it connects 'they look' (يَنظُرُونَ) with 'the camels' (ٱلْإِبِلِ), showing what they are looking at.
This root relates to camels and camel-related concepts
The vowel marks make this the common plural word for camels
Added ال to point to specific camels being discussed in this ayah

Added kasra at the end because this word follows إِلَى (to/towards) in the ayah. The whole phrase إِلَى ٱلْإِبِلِ means 'towards the camels' and is what they are looking at (يَنظُرُونَ means 'they look')

steps.m: how (asking about the way خُلِقَتْ/it was created)
steps.n: This is a question word used to ask about the way something happens. In this ayah, it's asking about how the camels were created.
Adding these vowel marks creates the proper pronunciation of this common question word

steps.m: to create or shape something from nothing
steps.n: This root relates to creation and making things
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Changed the vowels to show this is happening to something rather than something doing it

steps.m: it was created (referring to الإبل/the camel)
steps.n: Added ت to show this refers to the feminine word الإبل (the camel) mentioned earlier in the ayah
This is a basic preposition that shows direction or destination
The و (and) connects this to the previous phrase, showing another thing to look at and reflect upon
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past-tense verb
The verb 'rose up' became a noun 'sky' by adding ء at the end, since the sky is high up above us
Adding ال makes the word definite, like adding 'the' in English
The kasra at the end shows it's being possessed or comes after a preposition. Here, the word comes after the preposition إلى (to/towards) which causes it to be in the genitive case.
This is a question word that asks about the way or manner something happens. In this ayah, it's asking about how the sky was raised.
Shows how to say 'how' with the correct vowels (ka-y-fa). Asks about the way Allah raised the sky.
The root letters describe the action of lifting or raising something
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past tense verb
Changed vowels to show this happened to something rather than something doing it

steps.m: it was raised (referring to السَّمَآءِ - the sky)
steps.n: Added ت with sukun at the end to show this refers to a feminine word (the sky) in the past tense
This is a basic preposition that shows direction or destination. In this ayah, it points towards 'the mountains' (ٱلْجِبَالِ)
The و (and) connects this phrase to previous signs of Allah's power mentioned in earlier ayahs
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form the basic noun
This is a broken plural form used for groups of mountains
Added ال to make it definite and ِ at the end because it comes after the word إِلَى (to) in the ayah

steps.m: how (asking about the manner of نُصِبَتْ - were erected)
steps.n: This is the basic question word used to ask about manner or state. In this ayah, it's asking about how the mountains were erected.

steps.m: how (asking about the manner of نُصِبَتْ - were erected)
steps.n: Added vowel marks to show correct pronunciation: fatha on ك, sukun on ي, and fatha at the end
The root becomes a noun meaning the result of being tired
Connected the root letters with fatha to make a past tense verb
Changed vowels to show this happened to the mountains rather than the mountains doing it

steps.m: it (feminine) was set up (referring to الْجِبَالِ/the mountains)
steps.n: Added ت to match with 'mountains' which is feminine in Arabic
This is a basic directional particle that shows movement or attention towards something. In this ayah, it's used with الْأَرْضِ (the earth) to mean 'to/towards the earth'.
Added و at the beginning to connect this phrase to what came before it, showing another thing being pointed out or examined
Added vowel marks to show pronunciation: fatha on first letter and sukun on second letter
Adding ال makes it refer to a specific earth/ground rather than any earth/ground
The kasra at the end shows this word follows إِلَى (to) in the phrase 'and to the earth'
This is a question word that always asks about the way something happens
Adding these vowel marks helps us pronounce the word correctly as 'kayf'
Basic past tense form
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb
Changed vowels to show this happened to something (the earth) rather than something doing it

steps.m: it (feminine) was spread flat (referring to الأرض/the earth)
steps.n: Added ت with sukun to show this refers to the earth (which is feminine in Arabic)
Connected the root letters with fatha to make it a past tense verb
The shadda doubles the middle letter to show doing something with extra effort

steps.m: so remind! (strongly) (connected to إِنَّمَآ أَنتَ مُذَكِّرٌ - you are only a reminder)
steps.n: Added فَ (meaning 'so/then') to connect this command with the next statement that explains the Prophet's role as a reminder
Adding shadda makes this word stronger, changing it from 'if' to a word that emphasizes what comes after it
Adding ما changes the meaning from 'indeed' to 'only'. This combination is used to limit something, like saying 'you are only a reminder' instead of 'you are a reminder'
The alif is written with a line over it (آ) when it comes at the end of a word. This doesn't change the meaning.
This is the basic form of the second person pronoun
Added vowel marks to show this means 'you' (speaking to one male person) who is doing the reminding/warning
Added shadda to middle letter to show intensive meaning of reminding
Added مُ at beginning to show who does the action, changed middle vowel to kasra, and added tanween at end to make it indefinite
Adding tanween makes it 'a' reminder instead of 'the' reminder
Added vowels to connect the letters and make the basic negative verb form
The weak letter ي drops out and تَ is added to show 'you'

steps.m: you certainly are not (over عَلَيْهِم - over them)
steps.n: The doubled ل (shown by ّ) makes the negation stronger. This word negates your authority over them (عَلَيْهِم)
This is a basic preposition showing something being above or over something else. In this ayah, it connects to هِم to show what is being acted upon.
The ى changes to يْ to connect with the next part. This is just a spelling change that doesn't affect the meaning.

steps.m: upon them (object of لَّسْتَ - you are not)
steps.n: Added هِم which means 'them' (referring to multiple people). This word connects to لَّسْتَ (you are not) to show who you are not over.
Adding ي makes it mean 'to actively control' rather than just 'oversee'
Added مُ at the beginning to show who does the action, and changed س to ص due to common spelling variation

steps.m: with/by one who exercises control (negated by لَّسْتَ)
steps.n: Added بِ at beginning to mean 'with/by' and tanwin at end. This word is negated by لَّسْتَ (you are not) in the ayah.

This is the base form of the exception particle that introduces an exclusion from a previous statement. In this ayah, it excludes a specific group (those who turn away and disbelieve) from a previous statement.

steps.m: except (those who turn away and disbelieve)
steps.n: Added shadda and alif at the end to complete the particle. This is the standard form used for making exceptions in Arabic.

This is a relative pronoun that refers to people. In this ayah, it refers to the person who turns away and disbelieves. It connects with the verbs تَوَلَّىٰ (turns away) and كَفَرَ (disbelieves) that come after it.

steps.m: to be close to or take charge of something
steps.n: The root letters show the basic idea of being close to something or taking control of it
Connected the root letters with vowels to make a basic past tense verb
The middle letter is doubled with shadda to show intensity, and the final ي changes to ى because it's at the end

steps.m: he turned himself away (subject of كَفَرَ)
steps.n: Added تَ at the beginning to show he did this action to himself. This word is connected to كَفَرَ (he disbelieved) in the ayah.

steps.m: he turned himself away (subject of كَفَرَ)
steps.n: Added the small alif (ٰ) to show the proper pronunciation of the final ى

steps.m: to reject or deny truth by covering it up
steps.n: The root gives the basic idea of covering or hiding something, which led to meaning denial of truth

steps.m: he rejected/denied (truth) (connected to تَوَلَّىٰ - turned away)
steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to show this happened in the past. This word is connected to تَوَلَّىٰ (turned away) in the ayah, showing two related actions of rejection.

steps.m: and he rejected/denied (truth) (connected to تَوَلَّىٰ - turned away)
steps.n: Added و at the start to mean 'and', connecting this rejection to the previous action of turning away (تَوَلَّىٰ)
Added shadda to middle letter to show intensity of punishment
Adding يُ at the start changes it from past (he punished) to present (he punishes)
ف means 'and then' or 'so'

steps.m: and then he punishes him severely (subject is اللَّهُ)
steps.n: The هُ at the end means 'him'. Allah (اللَّهُ) is doing the punishing.
Changed the root into a noun meaning 'deity'
Added the u-mark at the end to show it's in nominative case
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make it a past tense verb
Changes from a verb to a noun meaning the punishment itself. This word is the direct object (what is being done) of the verb يُعَذِّبُ which means 'he punishes'.
Makes the noun definite by adding the definite article
Changed the initial alif to a wasl form since it comes after another word
Connected the root letters with fatha to make it a past tense verb
Added أ at start and changed vowels to make it mean 'more/bigger than'
Added ال to make it definite and fatha at end because it describes 'the punishment' in the ayah. When أَكْبَر has ال, it means 'the greatest' not just 'bigger'
This is a special word that has no root. It's like adding 'definitely' or 'truly' at the start of a sentence to make it stronger.
Added the kasra (إِ) and shadda (نَّ) marks to show how to pronounce it correctly. The shadda makes the ن sound stronger.
This is a basic preposition showing direction or destination. Since it's a particle, we start with its basic form rather than a root.
Added sukun (ْ) before adding a suffix. This is a spelling change that happens with this word.

steps.m: to us/towards us (connected to إِيَابَ - their return)
steps.n: Added نَآ at the end to mean 'us'. The long alif (آ) shows this word is connected to إِيَابَ (their return) in the ayah.
This root is about the action of returning or coming back to a place
Connected the root letters together to make a basic noun form
Changed the first letter to إِ and the middle weak letter و to يَا to make a special type of noun

steps.m: their return (connected to إِلَيْنَآ - to us)
steps.n: Added هُمْ at the end to show it's their return. This word connects to إِلَيْنَآ (to us) in the ayah, showing their return is to Allah.
This is a common connecting word that links events or ideas in order. The shadda (doubled م) gives it emphasis.
Added the dot (damma) above to show how to pronounce it correctly as 'thumma'

steps.m: then (connects to إِنَّ عَلَيْنَا حِسَابَهُم - indeed, upon Us is their account)
steps.n: Added shadda to emphasize that this is a strong connection to what comes next. In this ayah, it connects to the statement that follows about Allah's reckoning.
This is the basic form of the word before emphasis is added. It shows uncertainty or conditions.
Adding the shadda (doubled ن) changes this from showing uncertainty to showing certainty. In this ayah, it emphasizes that the reckoning/account (حِسَابَهُم) is definitely upon Us (عَلَيْنَا).
This is a basic preposition showing something is on top of or affecting something else
Added ي and sukun (ْ) to prepare for attaching the pronoun
Added نا at the end to show who is being affected by the preposition (us)
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past-tense verb
Changed the vowels and added an alif to make it mean the act of calculating
Added a fatha at the end because it follows the word عَلَيْنَا (upon us)
Added هُم at the end to show it's their reckoning
